content.assessment: This proposal extends an already-precedented administrative tool (discretionary refusal on segregation grounds) across all maintainer types, offering a feasibility advantage over purely legislative alternatives by building incrementally on existing practice rather than requiring new law. Its principal political exposure is the likely resistance from church and private maintainers, whose partial legal exemption from state jurisdiction creates a plausible legal and political challenge to any extension of ministerial discretion over their admissions and expansion decisions, and whose growth has occurred under the same governing coalition's tenure.
opportunities: Advocacy groups already actively monitor and publicize ministry decisions on segregation grounds, providing a ready constituency to sustain public legitimacy and pressure for consistent application
strengths: Builds directly on a demonstrated legal/administrative precedent (Tiszavasvári refusal) and a documented causal case (Encs creaming), giving the mechanism both operational grounding and a public-legitimacy narrative rooted in concrete harm

An existing 2023 legal channel already extended uniform terms across maintainer types, suggesting some institutional pathway for cross-sector regulatory reach already exists and could be built upon incrementally rather than via wholly new legislation
threats: Church and private maintainers, currently expanding their selective share and partially exempt from state maintenance jurisdiction, are likely to contest extended ministerial discretion as institutional-autonomy infringement, creating both political and potential legal resistance
Politically selective or inconsistent application of a segregation-impact test could invite accusations of arbitrary discretion, weakening the tool's durability and legitimacy over time
weaknesses: As a purely preventive tool, it cannot address segregation from already-approved tracks, limiting its political payoff to marginal future cases only, which may weaken the coalition of support among those seeking to address existing, not just future, segregation

Discretionary application inherently risks being perceived as politically selective, which could undermine legitimacy particularly if applied asymmetrically across state versus church/private maintainers
